---
title: "Workshop 4: Professional Reporting in Quarto"
subtitle: "Figures, Tables, and Cross-Referencing"
author: "Michael Lydeamore"

## 🎯 Objectives

[cite_start]Building on our ability to sync repositories and manage branches[cite: 1, 9], today we focus on the technical side of professional reporting:

* [cite_start]Create a Quarto report with a table, figures, and sections that are labelled and referenced [cite: 1]
* Generate professional figures with captions and sub-figures
* Use **cross-referencing** to link text to your figures and tables dynamically
* (Extension) Implement **academic referencing** using BibTeX in Quarto

---

## 🖼️ Task 1: Working with Figures (15 minutes)

In Quarto, figures are more than just images; they are numbered entities with metadata. [cite_start]To make a figure "referencable," its label **must** start with the prefix `fig-`[cite: 1].

### Exercise
1. Create a new code chunk in your QMD.
2. Use the following code to generate a plot:


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![Body mass vs. Flipper length for three penguin species.](tutorial-04_files/figure-html/fig-penguins-scatter-1.png){#fig-penguins-scatter width=672}
:::
:::


> **Pro-Tip:** The `#| label: fig-xxx` chunk option is vital. [cite_start]Without the `fig-` prefix, Quarto will not include this in the automatic "List of Figures" or allow for dynamic cross-references[cite: 1].

---

## 📊 Task 2: Creating Professional Tables (15 minutes)

[cite_start]Static Markdown tables are fine for simple notes, but for reproducible research, we generate tables directly from data frames[cite: 1]. To cross-reference a table, the label **must** start with `tbl-`.

### Exercise
Create a summary table of the penguin data using `knitr::kable`:


::: {#tbl-penguin-summary .cell tbl-cap='Mean body mass of penguins by species and sex.'}
::: {.cell-output-display}


|species   |sex    | mean_mass|
|:---------|:------|---------:|
|Adelie    |female |  3368.836|
|Adelie    |male   |  4043.493|
|Chinstrap |female |  3527.206|
|Chinstrap |male   |  3938.971|
|Gentoo    |female |  4679.741|
|Gentoo    |male   |  5484.836|


:::
:::


---

## 🔗 Task 3: Dynamic Cross-Referencing (15 minutes)

One of the greatest strengths of Quarto is automation. [cite_start]If you add a new figure at the beginning of your document, Quarto renumbers every subsequent figure and update every mention in your text automatically[cite: 1].

### Syntax
* To reference a figure: Use `@fig-label`
* To reference a table: Use `@tbl-label`

### Exercise
Write a short paragraph in your document referencing the items you created above:

> As shown in @fig-penguins-scatter, there is a clear positive correlation between flipper length and body mass. The specific averages for each group are detailed in @tbl-penguin-summary.

---

## 🎓 Extension: Academic Referencing (15 minutes)

To use academic citations, you need a bibliography file (usually a `.bib` file). 

### 1. Create a Bibliography
Create a new text file in your project folder named `references.bib` and paste this BibTeX entry inside:

```bibtex
@Article{penguins_data,
  title = {Low-Level Admixture and Potential Barrier to Gene Flow in Gentoo Penguins},
  author = {Gorman, Kristen B. and Williams, Tony D. and Fraser, William R.},
  journal = {PLOS ONE},
  year = {2014},
  volume = {9},
  number = {3}
}
```

### 2. Update your YAML
Add the `bibliography` field to the top of your `.qmd` file:

```yaml
---
title: "Workshop 4"
bibliography: references.bib
---
```

### 3. Cite in Text
Use the key from your `.bib` file preceded by an `@` symbol.

* **Parenthetical:** The data was originally collected by Gorman et al. [@penguins_data].
* **Narrative:** @penguins_data argues that...

Quarto will automatically generate a **References** section at the very end of your document.

---

## 🧹 Summary Checklist
- [ ] Do all my figure labels start with `fig-`?
- [ ] Do all my table labels start with `tbl-`?
- [ ] Did I use the `@` symbol to link to them in my text?
- [ ] (Extension) Is my `.bib` file saved in the same folder as my `.qmd`?
